findings.description: Comprehensive GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with matrix testing across multiple Python versions
findings.evidence: Found 5+ workflow files in .github/workflows/ including python_matrix.yml which runs matrix testing across Python 3.12, 3.13, and 3.14. Workflow includes concurrency control, permission restrictions (contents: read), dependency caching, and runs tests with -Wall flag. Workflow configuration: 'name: Python Matrix from config file, on: pull_request, types: [labeled, synchronize, opened, reopened], timeout-minutes: 60'
findings.recommendation: Continue current practices. The CI/CD setup is exemplary with modern features like dynamic version detection from pyproject.toml, proper concurrency management, and comprehensive test coverage.

findings.description: Active and recent CI/CD usage with daily merge activity
findings.evidence: Last 10 commits from April 18-22, 2026 show daily merge activity. Recent commits include: '61a62be313e395ce1265132bfc99f51476fb3c95' (Apr 22), '63c56cda133a85a158502891c40465bc0331d3d9' (Apr 22), 'dc467fdc3b5744cec71fab876c23a14013e2510b' (Apr 22), indicating same-day commit and merge patterns
findings.recommendation: No action required. CI pipeline is actively used and processing changes daily.

findings.description: Comprehensive linting and formatting tooling configured
findings.evidence: Repository contains multiple quality control files: .flake8 (297 bytes), .editorconfig (697 bytes), .pre-commit-config.yaml (797 bytes), biome.json (1699 bytes for JavaScript). pyproject.toml contains [tool.black] with target-version=['py312'] and [tool.isort] with profile='black'
findings.recommendation: Continue enforcing pre-commit hooks and automated formatting. Consider adding automated checks in CI to reject PRs that don't pass linting.

findings.description: Excellent PR velocity with same-day merges for simple changes and appropriate review time for complex features
findings.evidence: Recent merged PRs show healthy patterns: PR #21152 opened and merged on Apr 22 (same day), PR #20962 opened Mar 21 and merged Apr 22 (1 month for complex fix), PR #19743 opened Aug 2025 and merged Apr 22 2026 (8 months for complex feature with selenium and coverage labels). Open PRs are fresh: PR #21154 (< 1 hour old), PR #21153 (< 1 hour old), PR #21148 (1 day old)
findings.recommendation: Maintain current review practices. The balance between speed for simple changes and thoroughness for complex features is optimal.

findings.description: Structured PR template enforcing quality standards and governance requirements
findings.evidence: .github/pull_request_template.md (1616 bytes) includes mandatory sections: Trac ticket number, AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED), comprehensive checklist including contribution guidelines compliance, commit message format, testing requirements, and documentation updates
findings.recommendation: The PR template is excellent. Consider adding a reminder about updating CHANGELOG/release notes for user-facing changes.

findings.description: No stale open PRs detected but monitoring recommended
findings.evidence: Top 10 open PRs show ages: 4 days (PR #21131), 3 days (PR #21138, #21137), 2 days (PR #21148, #21145, #21144), 1 day (PR #21150), < 1 day (PR #21154, #21153). All appear actively developed with recent updates
findings.recommendation: Implement automated stale PR detection to flag PRs older than 30-60 days without activity. This proactive measure will help maintain current excellent standards.

findings.description: Exemplary repository organization with clear separation of concerns
findings.evidence: Root contains 28 items with proper structure: django/ (main code), tests/ (test suite), docs/ (documentation), js_tests/ (JavaScript tests), scripts/ (utilities), .github/ (CI/CD), plus standard files like README.rst, AUTHORS (45163 bytes), CONTRIBUTING.rst, LICENSE, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, SECURITY.md, FUNDING.yml
findings.recommendation: No changes needed. Structure follows best practices for large-scale open source projects.

findings.description: Modern Python packaging with minimal, well-specified dependencies
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml uses PEP 621 format with [build-system] requires=['setuptools>=77.0.3'], requires-python='>= 3.12', dependencies=['asgiref>=3.9.1', 'sqlparse>=0.5.0', 'tzdata; sys_platform == win32'], and optional-dependencies for argon2 and bcrypt. Includes [tool.black] and [tool.isort] configurations
findings.recommendation: Consider implementing automated dependency update tooling like Dependabot or Renovate to track updates to asgiref, sqlparse, and build dependencies.

findings.description: Comprehensive test suite with multiple testing configurations
findings.evidence: Repository contains dedicated tests/ directory, js_tests/ for JavaScript, tox.ini (2195 bytes) for multi-environment testing. CI workflow shows: 'run: python -Wall tests/runtests.py -v2' with cache-dependency-path: 'tests/requirements/py3.txt'. Recent PRs show selenium and coverage labels indicating integration and coverage testing
findings.recommendation: Excellent testing setup. Consider publishing test coverage metrics publicly to increase transparency and encourage contributions.

findings.description: Strong community governance with transparency requirements ahead of industry standards
findings.evidence: PR template mandates AI Assistance Disclosure with checkbox: '**No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR' or disclosure of which tools were used. Multiple governance files present: CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md (70 bytes), SECURITY.md (80 bytes), FUNDING.yml (68 bytes), CONTRIBUTING.rst (1115 bytes)
findings.recommendation: Continue current governance practices. The mandatory AI disclosure policy is progressive and sets a good example for the open source community.

findings.description: Well-documented project with integrated documentation infrastructure
findings.evidence: Dedicated docs/ directory, .readthedocs.yml (410 bytes) for ReadTheDocs integration, README.rst (2017 bytes), comprehensive AUTHORS file (45163 bytes tracking contributors), project.urls in pyproject.toml includes Homepage, Documentation, Release notes, Funding, Source, and Tracker
findings.recommendation: Documentation infrastructure is excellent. No changes needed.

findings.description: Good security practices in workflows with locked permissions
findings.evidence: python_matrix.yml includes 'permissions: contents: read' restricting workflow permissions, 'persist-credentials: false' in checkout actions, and 'concurrency: group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: true' preventing resource exhaustion
findings.recommendation: Current security posture is strong. Consider adding SAST (Static Application Security Testing) workflows for automated vulnerability scanning of dependencies and code.

mcp_params.query: repo:django/django filename:CHANGELOG OR filename:HISTORY OR filename:RELEASE

findings.description: GitHub Issues are disabled in favor of external Trac system. CONTRIBUTING.rst states: 'Warning: non-trivial pull requests (anything more than fixing a typo) without Trac tickets will be closed! Django uses Trac to keep track of bugs, feature requests, and associated patches because GitHub doesn't provide adequate tooling for its community.'
findings.evidence: list_issues returned 0 open and 0 closed issues. CONTRIBUTING.rst content: 'Django uses Trac to keep track of bugs...Patches can be submitted as pull requests, but if you don't file a ticket, it's unlikely that we'll notice your contribution.'
findings.recommendation: Add a .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE.md that redirects users to https://code.djangoproject.com/newticket with clear instructions to reduce confusion for GitHub-native contributors.

findings.description: No GitHub issue templates found, which may confuse new contributors expecting standard GitHub workflows.
findings.evidence: search_code query for 'repo:django/django path:.github/ filename:ISSUE_TEMPLATE' returned total_count: 0, incomplete_results: false
findings.recommendation: Create issue template files that explain the Trac-first workflow, even if GitHub Issues remain disabled.

findings.description: Strong contributor diversity with 9 distinct authors in the last 10 commits, indicating healthy community participation and low bus factor risk.
findings.evidence: list_commits (perPage=10) showed authors: charettes, annalauraw, DineshThumma9, aadeina, Artyom Kotovskiy (2 commits), cliffordgama, felixxm, jacobtylerwalls, varunkasyap. Committers primarily jacobtylerwalls (8/10) and sarahboyce (1/10), showing active maintainer engagement.
findings.recommendation: Continue current practices. Consider highlighting top monthly contributors in release notes or community updates.

findings.description: Active maintainer integration with fast merge velocity. Jacob Walls appears as committer in 8 of 10 recent commits, demonstrating consistent review and merge activity.
findings.evidence: Commits from April 19-22, 2026 show rapid integration: commit 673fa46d (Apr 18 authored, Apr 19 committed), commit 5cf9c7f (Apr 19 authored and committed same day), multiple commits merged within 2-4 days of authorship.
findings.recommendation: Monitor maintainer workload distribution to prevent burnout. Consider expanding the merge rights to additional trusted contributors.

findings.description: Comprehensive governance documentation present including CONTRIBUTING.rst (1,115 bytes) with clear contribution guidelines and Code of Conduct reference.
findings.evidence: get_file_contents returned CONTRIBUTING.rst with sections on contribution types, extensive guidelines reference at docs/internals/contributing/, and CoC link: https://www.djangoproject.com/conduct/. File also includes AUTHORS file (45,163 bytes) for contributor recognition.
findings.recommendation: Governance is excellent. Consider adding a GOVERNANCE.md in the root to make the Django Software Foundation's decision-making structure more discoverable.

findings.description: Code of Conduct exists and is clearly referenced, though hosted externally on djangoproject.com rather than in the repository.
findings.evidence: CONTRIBUTING.rst states: 'As a contributor, you can help us keep the Django community open and inclusive. Please read and follow our Code of Conduct <https://www.djangoproject.com/conduct/>.'
findings.recommendation: External hosting is acceptable for established foundations. No action required, though a CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md stub with link could improve discoverability.

findings.description: Commit messages demonstrate active issue tracking integration with numbered references and collaborative authorship patterns.
findings.evidence: Recent commits show structured references: 'Fixed #37057', 'Fixed #35870', 'Fixed #36991', 'Fixed #15759', 'Refs #36005', 'Refs #373, #34122'. Commit 63c56cda shows co-authorship: 'Co-Authored-By: Marijke Luttekes <mail@marijkeluttekes.dev>'. Commit e1bdebc shows multiple co-authors.
findings.recommendation: Continue current practices. The Trac integration and co-authorship patterns demonstrate strong collaboration.

findings.description: Professional release management infrastructure with documented processes and automation tooling.
findings.evidence: search_code found release-related files: 'docs/internals/release-process.txt', 'docs/internals/howto-release-django.txt', 'scripts/do_django_release.py', 'scripts/verify_release.sh'. get_repository_tree confirmed presence of comprehensive docs/ directory structure.
findings.recommendation: Release process is mature and well-documented. Consider adding a RELEASES.md in root linking to the versioned release notes for quick reference.

findings.description: No standalone CHANGELOG.md in repository root, though changes are documented in versioned release notes in the docs system.
findings.evidence: search_code for 'filename:CHANGELOG OR filename:HISTORY' returned 7 results, all related to release documentation in docs/internals/ and scripts/, but no root-level CHANGELOG.md file.
findings.recommendation: For improved GitHub discoverability, consider adding a CHANGELOG.md that links to the comprehensive release notes at https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/releases/.

findings.description: Extensive contributor recognition through AUTHORS file and co-authorship practices in commit messages.
findings.evidence: get_repository_tree showed AUTHORS file at 45,163 bytes. Multiple recent commits use Co-Authored-By tags: commit 63c56cda with Marijke Luttekes, commit e1bdebc with Peter van der Does and Jacob Walls.
findings.recommendation: Excellent practices already in place. Continue using co-authorship tags and maintaining the AUTHORS file.

findings.description: README.rst provides comprehensive project overview with all essential information
findings.evidence: README.rst (2,017 bytes, SHA: b7532e6b5ad352fe5d560962f8c59356d2424fae) contains: project description, documentation pointers (docs/intro/install.txt, docs/intro/tutorial01.txt through tutorial08.txt), contribution links (https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/), deployment guide (docs/howto/deployment/index.txt), testing instructions, community resources (Discord, Forum), and Django Software Foundation support information.
findings.recommendation: Continue maintaining this exemplary README structure as the gold standard.

findings.description: Extensive Sphinx-based documentation with comprehensive structure
findings.evidence: docs/ directory contains: conf.py (14,810 bytes Sphinx config), Makefile (7,125 bytes build system), README.rst (655 bytes build instructions), .readthedocs.yml (410 bytes ReadTheDocs integration). Subdirectories include: docs/ref/ (API reference), docs/topics/ (topical guides), docs/howto/ (how-to guides), docs/faq/ (FAQ), docs/internals/ (internal documentation), docs/man/ (man pages). Search found 52+ __init__.py files indicating well-organized module structure.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current documentation structure and continue publishing to docs.djangoproject.com.

findings.description: Exceptional release documentation spanning 18+ years with hundreds of versioned files
findings.evidence: docs/releases/ directory contains comprehensive release notes from version 0.95 through 6.1, including: index.txt (6,172 bytes index), security.txt (70,747 bytes security history), major versions (1.0.txt, 2.2.txt, 3.2.txt LTS, 4.2.txt LTS, 5.0.txt 26,986 bytes, 5.1.txt 18,631 bytes, 5.2.txt 17,839 bytes, 6.0.txt 25,030 bytes, 6.1.txt 18,516 bytes), and all patch releases (e.g., 6.0.5.txt, 5.2.13.txt, 5.1.15.txt, 4.2.30.txt). All files use consistent reStructuredText format.
findings.recommendation: Continue this excellent release documentation practice with each version.

findings.description: Multi-layered contributing documentation with process clarity and Code of Conduct
findings.evidence: CONTRIBUTING.rst (1,115 bytes, SHA: 4b2ab363660df5b4b43e8e263b70ddb96b1db54e) provides overview and links to docs/intro/contributing.txt (23,718 bytes extended guide). Content covers: contribution types (code patches, documentation improvements, bug reports, patch reviews), Trac workflow requirement, explicit warning about PR requirements, Code of Conduct link (https://www.djangoproject.com/conduct/), and comprehensive guidelines at docs/internals/contributing/.
findings.recommendation: Keep the clear Trac workflow explanation to manage contributor expectations.

findings.description: Comprehensive 8-part tutorial series with progressive learning path
findings.evidence: docs/intro/ directory contains extensive tutorial series: tutorial01.txt (10,844 bytes - getting started), tutorial02.txt (26,180 bytes - database/admin), tutorial03.txt (18,075 bytes - views/templates), tutorial04.txt (14,218 bytes - forms), tutorial05.txt (28,460 bytes - testing), tutorial06.txt (5,508 bytes - static files), tutorial07.txt (16,289 bytes - admin customization), tutorial08.txt (4,707 bytes - third-party packages). Additional resources: overview.txt (12,922 bytes with code examples), install.txt (2,808 bytes), reusable-apps.txt (14,368 bytes advanced tutorial), whatsnext.txt (9,095 bytes guidance), and _images/ directory for visual aids.
findings.recommendation: Continue expanding tutorials to cover new Django features as they are released.

findings.description: Repository has a security policy file that references Django's comprehensive security program at https://www.djangoproject.com/security/. The project maintains a well-established responsible disclosure process with a dedicated security team, clear reporting channels, and public CVE assignment.
findings.evidence: File .github/SECURITY.md exists (SHA: 58d191de54b8b93de9c67ebbc1fa0b5d183b2921) with content: '# Django Security Policies\n\nPlease see https://www.djangoproject.com/security/.'
findings.recommendation: Maintain current security policy. Consider adding a brief summary of the disclosure process directly in SECURITY.md for quick reference before redirecting to the full policy.

findings.description: The repository lacks Python-specific security scanning tools in CI/CD. While linters.yml workflow includes code quality tools (flake8, isort, black, biome), no dedicated SAST tools like CodeQL, Bandit, or Semgrep are configured. The workflow does include zizmor for GitHub Actions security scanning.
findings.evidence: Workflow .github/workflows/linters.yml (SHA: ccee1adde022eccc84a9b95bce82294161d4212c) contains flake8, isort, black, zizmor, and biome. Search query 'repo:django/django path:.github/workflows codeql' returned 0 results, indicating no CodeQL workflow present.
findings.recommendation: Implement CodeQL analysis for Python or integrate Bandit/Semgrep into the CI pipeline. CodeQL is free for open-source projects and provides comprehensive security vulnerability detection for Python codebases.

findings.description: Unable to verify the presence of automated dependency vulnerability scanning configuration. No Dependabot configuration file could be retrieved, and no evidence of Renovate or similar tools was found in public workflows.
findings.evidence: Attempted to retrieve .github/dependabot.yml resulted in stream error. Review of 19 workflow files (docs.yml, labels.yml, schedules.yml, new_contributor_pr.yml, python_matrix.yml, benchmark.yml, linters.yml, check_commit_messages.yml, check_pr_quality.yml, check-migrations.yml) showed no dependency scanning workflows.
findings.recommendation: Verify Dependabot is enabled and properly configured with .github/dependabot.yml. Consider adding visible dependency update automation and documenting the dependency security process in SECURITY.md.

findings.description: Code search reveals minimal use of potentially dangerous patterns. Only 2 instances of 'shell=True' found in the entire Python codebase, both in utility/administrative contexts rather than production code paths.
findings.evidence: Search 'repo:django/django shell=True language:python' returned 2 results: django/utils/version.py (SHA: be727df7b6e37001c8074ad07b931e6634028f35) and scripts/manage_translations.py (SHA: ce3093ca4a51b327977c192634e5f26b40bd1148). Both appear to be non-user-facing utility code.
findings.recommendation: Continue current practices. Consider adding explicit code review guidelines for subprocess calls and dynamic code execution patterns.

findings.description: Mixed GitHub Actions pinning strategy observed. Some third-party actions use secure SHA pinning (liskin/gh-problem-matcher-wrap, zizmorcore/zizmor-action), but official GitHub actions use only semantic version tags (actions/checkout@v6, actions/setup-python@v6, actions/setup-node@v5, psf/black@stable), which are vulnerable to tag manipulation attacks.
findings.evidence: From .github/workflows/linters.yml: SHA-pinned actions include 'liskin/gh-problem-matcher-wrap@e7b7beaaafa52524748b31a381160759d68d61fb' and 'zizmorcore/zizmor-action@e639db99335bc9038abc0e066dfcd72e23d26fb4'. Tag-pinned actions include 'actions/checkout@v6', 'actions/setup-python@v6', 'actions/setup-node@v5', 'psf/black@stable'.
findings.recommendation: Pin all GitHub Actions to full commit SHAs to prevent tag tampering attacks. Use tools like Dependabot to keep pinned actions up-to-date automatically.

findings.description: The repository uses zizmor, a specialized security scanner for GitHub Actions workflows, indicating awareness of supply chain security in CI/CD pipelines. The linters workflow also includes appropriate permission restrictions with 'permissions: contents: read'.
findings.evidence: Workflow .github/workflows/linters.yml includes 'zizmorcore/zizmor-action@e639db99335bc9038abc0e066dfcd72e23d26fb4' and sets 'permissions: contents: read' at the workflow level.
findings.recommendation: Continue using zizmor and maintain minimal permissions. Ensure all workflows follow the principle of least privilege for GitHub token permissions.

findings.description: Security-conscious workflow configuration detected with proper permission restrictions and credential handling. The linters workflow uses 'persist-credentials: false' in checkout steps and sets minimal read-only permissions.
findings.evidence: Multiple steps in .github/workflows/linters.yml include 'persist-credentials: false' parameter in actions/checkout steps, and workflow-level 'permissions: contents: read' restricts token scope.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current security practices. Consider documenting these security patterns as requirements for new workflow contributions.

findings.description: Primary LICENSE file present with clear BSD 3-Clause license text. Copyright holder is 'Django Software Foundation and individual contributors'. License is legally complete and unmodified.
findings.evidence: LICENSE file found at repository root (SHA: 5f4f225dd282aa7e4361ec3c2750bbbaaed8ab1f). Content begins: 'Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation and individual contributors. All rights reserved. Redistribution and use in source and binary forms, with or without modification, are permitted provided that the following conditions are met...' Full BSD 3-Clause text present.
findings.recommendation: No action required. License documentation is exemplary.

findings.description: License properly declared in project metadata using SPDX identifier 'BSD-3-Clause'. Multiple license files explicitly referenced in pyproject.toml.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml (SHA: c64cd28dab46fc6f1a24ef3e963cd6bd54186c93) contains: [project] license = "BSD-3-Clause" and license-files = ["LICENSE", "LICENSE.python", "AUTHORS"]. SPDX identifier ensures machine-readable compliance.
findings.recommendation: Continue maintaining license-files declaration when adding third-party code.

findings.description: Exemplary third-party attribution for embedded Python standard library code. Dedicated LICENSE.python file contains full Python Software Foundation License Version 2 plus historical licenses (BeOpen, CNRI, CWI).
findings.evidence: LICENSE.python file present (SHA: 2fc28e876fa1fc8c536f46b81789e2bb51b323c0, 14256 bytes). Preamble states: 'Django is licensed under the three-clause BSD license; see the file LICENSE for details. Django includes code from the Python standard library, which is licensed under the Python license, a permissive open source license.' Full PSF License V2 text follows.
findings.recommendation: Maintain this best-practice approach for any future embedded third-party code.

findings.description: AUTHORS file present with comprehensive contributor recognition (45KB), supporting BSD license requirement to retain copyright notices and acknowledge contributors.
findings.evidence: AUTHORS file found in repository tree (SHA: 07c87068a9d3b46491391818053839dcd5ffcf1e, size: 45163 bytes). Referenced in pyproject.toml license-files declaration.
findings.recommendation: Continue maintaining AUTHORS file as contributors are added.

findings.description: No per-file copyright/license headers found in Python source files. While acceptable for BSD-licensed projects (not required like GPL), headers improve per-file clarity.
findings.evidence: Code search query 'repo:django/django "Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation" language:python' returned 0 results. Sample file django/__init__.py (SHA: 4402600c576fab7d8e88389799558aa963369b2d) contains no license header, begins directly with code.
findings.recommendation: Optional: Consider adding SPDX-License-Identifier headers to source files for enhanced clarity. Format: '# Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation and individual contributors.\n# SPDX-License-Identifier: BSD-3-Clause'. Priority: Low (cosmetic only).

findings.description: All declared dependencies use permissive licenses compatible with BSD 3-Clause. No copyleft dependencies detected that could create licensing conflicts.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml dependencies: asgiref>=3.9.1 (BSD), sqlparse>=0.5.0 (BSD), tzdata (public domain/permissive), argon2-cffi>=23.1.0 (MIT), bcrypt>=4.1.1 (Apache 2.0). All are compatible with BSD 3-Clause license.
findings.recommendation: Continue vetting dependency licenses during updates to ensure compatibility.

findings.description: Repository tree shows comprehensive license-related files at root level: LICENSE, LICENSE.python, AUTHORS, CONTRIBUTING.rst, README.rst. Clear separation between Django license and third-party attributions.
findings.evidence: Repository tree (28 items, non-recursive) includes: LICENSE (blob, 1552 bytes), LICENSE.python (blob, 14256 bytes), AUTHORS (blob, 45163 bytes), CONTRIBUTING.rst (blob, 1115 bytes), README.rst (blob, 2017 bytes). All license files accessible at root level.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current structure. This is a best-practice example for open-source projects.

findings.description: Modern packaging metadata follows PEP 621 standards with explicit license file inclusion and SPDX identifier. Ensures license files are distributed with packages.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml uses [project] table with license = "BSD-3-Clause" (SPDX) and license-files list. Build system configured with setuptools>=77.0.3. Package metadata includes authors, description, and project URLs including funding and source links.
findings.recommendation: No changes needed. Current configuration ensures compliance in distributed packages.
